narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the unique challenges you've likely navigated to get here. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an undergraduate GPA of 2.70, an LSAT score of 32, a middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated your probability of failing at 65.9%—a threshold that led to the regrettable rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking at the bottom, which stood out starkly against the typical student's mid-range performance and signaled potential struggles in sustaining academic momentum. Your first-year decile in the lowest group also contributed notably in the same direction, differing from the average student's stronger start. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA, though below the dataset average of 3.21, helped pull the prediction somewhat toward passing by demonstrating a baseline of college-level capability. Similarly, your LSAT score, while lower than the typical 36.7, offered a modest counterbalance, suggesting some aptitude for legal reasoning. Your full-time enrollment plans aligned with the vast majority of successful students and provided a small boost toward a passing prediction. Family income in the middle bracket had minimal sway either way compared to averages. Overall, your academic decile rankings, especially in later years, were the dominant drivers outweighing these positives, placing you below typical admitted students who cluster in higher deciles with stronger GPAs and LSATs. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with updated achievements that align more closely with high performers. You're resilient, and with targeted improvements, you can shift this narrative in your favor—we believe in your potential.
